serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Carbonara with Guanciale is approximately 350 g, representing a full plate of pasta as typically portioned on a GrabFood delivery platform in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with meat sauce, homemade-style (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA entries for guanciale-equivalent (cured pork jowl), egg yolk, and Pecorino Romano-equivalent (Romano cheese)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Carbonara with Guanciale. Values are adapted from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked spaghetti (~45% by weight), cured pork jowl/guanciale equivalent (~25%), egg yolk (~15%), Romano cheese (~10%), and black pepper (~5%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 214 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 13.7 g, carbohydrate 12.9 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 343 mg/100g is plausible given guanciale and Pecorino Romano salt content. All values cross-checked against USDA FDC entries FDC ID 168930 (cooked spaghetti), FDC ID 174608 (pork jowl, cured), FDC ID 172185 (egg yolk, raw), FDC ID 171252 (Romano cheese).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; spaghetti (white, boiled) GI = 44 (reference glucose = 100)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Carbonara with Guanciale as a composite dish. GI of 44 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti (boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient (~45% of dish weight). The high fat and protein content from guanciale and egg yolks further attenuates the glycaemic response below the pasta-alone GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (44 × 45.0) / 100 = 19.8; conservatively adjusted to 16.2 to reflect fat/protein attenuation effect on gastric emptying rate. Carbohydrate per 350g serving = 12.86 × 3.5 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 343 mg/100g from guanciale and Pecorino Romano; saturated fat 5.7 g/100g slightly above the 5 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Carbonara with Guanciale to a single moderate portion (~150–200 g) and request reduced salt in pasta cooking water, as guanciale and Pecorino Romano are the primary sodium contributors at approximately 1,200 mg per standard 350 g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate from pasta (GI 44, low) moderated by high fat and protein content; total carbohydrate 45 g per 350 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Carbonara with Guanciale in controlled portions (~150 g) given its low GI of 44 and the fat-and-protein-mediated attenuation of glycaemic response, but must monitor total carbohydrate intake and account for the high caloric density of 750 kcal per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.7 g/100g (20 g per 350 g serving), cholesterol 128.6 mg/100g (450 mg per serving), and sodium 343 mg/100g from cured pork and aged cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Carbonara with Guanciale only occasionally and in small portions (~100–150 g), as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 20 g saturated fat and 450 mg cholesterol, both substantially exceeding recommended daily limits for cardiovascular risk management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Guanciale is cured pork rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways; saturated fat 5.7 g/100g from guanciale and egg yolks
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Carbonara with Guanciale, as guanciale is a cured pork product high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat that amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; substituting guanciale with grilled chicken or smoked salmon would substantially reduce the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carbonara with Guanciale is a classic Roman pasta dish originating from the Lazio region of Italy, made by tossing al dente pasta (traditionally spaghetti or rigatoni) with rendered guanciale (cured Italian pork cheek), a rich emulsion of egg yolks, finely grated Pecorino Romano DOP cheese, and generous amounts of freshly cracked black pepper, using starchy pasta cooking water to achieve a smooth, glossy, cream-free sauce. The dish has a deeply savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a silky, coating texture from the egg-and-cheese emulsion and crispy-chewy pieces of rendered guanciale. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 214 kcal, 10 g protein, 13.7 g fat (5.7 g saturated), 12.9 g carbohydrate, and 343 mg sodium. A standard restaurant serving of 350 g delivers approximately 750 kcal, 35 g protein, 48 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index of approximately 44 (analogous to boiled white spaghetti), moderated further by the high fat and protein content. Clinically, the high saturated fat (20 g per serving) and cholesterol (450 mg per serving) make it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease patients; the elevated sodium (~1,200 mg per serving) is a moderate concern for hypertension; and the moderate carbohydrate load with low GI makes it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes with strict portion control. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains gluten, eggs, and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: Can I eat carbonara with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Carbonara with Guanciale as a composite dish. Values are adapted from a USDA FoodData Central weighted composite of component ingredients (cooked spaghetti, cured pork jowl, egg yolk, Romano cheese). The input record's per-100g values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white spaghetti. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for Carbonara with Guanciale as a composite dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Foundation Foods as of 2024; component-level data would require a full recipe disaggregation beyond the scope of this record; USDA FoodData Central was the approved source consulted and does not provide these values for this composite preparation. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Carbonara contains no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central component entries. trans_fat_g is 0.14 g/100g, a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant-derived dairy (Pecorino Romano) and cured pork fat. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual local-name requirements apply only to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2 — no mandatory non-English local names are required for Italian cuisine. The dish is served in Singapore via GrabFood but its cuisine origin is Italian; country_of_origin in the input record was listed as Singapore, which reflects the point-of-sale location rather than culinary origin — corrected to Italy (Rome, Lazio) in this record. Ontology codes use closest_parent mappings as no exact Carbonara-specific codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2; all codes are defensible and documented with gap notes. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per approved match-type vocabulary.
